pair.title: Show HN: GoModel – an open-source AI gateway in Go

story.title: GitHub - ENTERPILOT/GoModel: AI gateway written in Go. Lightweight unified OpenAI-compatible API for OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Groq, xAI & Ollama. LiteLLM alternative with observability, guardrails, streaming, costs and usage tracking.

comments.text: How does this actually compare to LiteLLM in terms of performance and feature parity? LiteLLM has been around longer and already handles most of these providers. What's the concrete advantage here beyond "written in Go"?

kids.text: I've used LiteLLM in production for 18 months across 4 different projects. The startup time on each request is noticeable (Python cold starts, dependency chain), and cost tracking can lag by minutes. Switched one service to GoModel last month and the latency dropped by 200

That 200ms drop is significant, but worth checking: is that cold-start latency or per-request? LiteLLM's Python overhead is mostly at import time. If you're running it in a persistent process (which most production setups do), the per-request delta shrinks substantially.

comments.text: We've been running Ollama + vLLM locally and needed something to route between them plus our OpenAI fallback. Built a quick wrapper in Python but this Go implementation looks cleaner. Main question: does it handle per-endpoint rate limiting and token budgets, or just pass-through aggregation? LiteL

kids.text: This looks solid for the multi-provider routing use case. The Go implementation should be significantly faster than a Python wrapper, especially under load. On your rate limiting question, I'd check the README or open an issue, LiteLLM does expose per-endpoint budgets, but I'm not sure if Go

This reminds me of the old API gateway days before everyone standardized on OpenAI's format. Back then you'd have 5 different wrapper layers just to talk to different LLM providers. The fact that we've converged on a common interface is genuinely good, but the fragmentation is cre

comments.text: We've been using LiteLLM in production for 18 months. The appeal of Go here is real if you're already in that ecosystem (faster cold starts, single binary, no Python runtime overhead). But LiteLLM's provider support matrix is genuinely wider and the retry/fallback

kids.text: Go single-binary deployments are genuinely nice for this. I've been evaluating both and GoModel's approach is cleaner if you're already containerized (which most people are). The observability hooks are a step above LiteLLM's baseline. LiteLLM still wins on breadth of integrations and the community is much larger, but if your provider mix is stable and you want faster iteration cycles, this is worth a look. The cost tracking piece in particular feels more baked-in here rather

comments.text: I've run LiteLLM in production for two years across 8 different model providers. The main friction points are request routing latency (adds ~40-80ms on average) and occasional desync when provider APIs change schemas. Go rewrite could help there, but I'd need to see actual

kids.text: Thanks for the real-world context on LiteLLM. The latency hit is something we've specifically optimized for in GoModel - we're seeing 8-15ms overhead on average in our benchmarks, mostly connection pooling and request multiplexing. Schema drift is trickier, but

We switched from LiteLLM to a custom Go gateway last year after hitting similar latency issues. The 40-80ms was killing our sub-100ms SLA on inference calls. We got it down to 12-18ms with connection reuse and careful goroutine pooling, so

LiteLLM's schema drift issues are real, but worth noting: the maintainer has added a compatibility layer for that exact problem in 0.1.340+. Still, the latency floor on Python is higher. Go might actually deliver the 20-30ms reduction you're looking for,

The compatibility layer in 0.1.340 is helpful, but it's a patch over a deeper problem: Python's request/response cycle just doesn't match the latency requirements of real-time gateway workloads. I ran some tests with LiteLLM against Groq (which demands <100ms round-trip)
